ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for golden pomfret seafood soup. FOODON_03301710 (fish soup food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish soup) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for golden pomfret seafood soup with mixed seafood components.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for golden pomfret seafood soup as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 500 g, comprising a bowl of broth with golden pomfret slices, pork balls, prawns, clams, and tomato, as typically served at Singapore seafood soup stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Soup (Mixed Seafood) — adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for mixed seafood broth-based soup
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for Golden Pomfret Seafood Soup. Values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo fish soup category entries (fish soup with mixed seafood, ~60–70 kcal/100 g range) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central raw golden pomfret (Trachinotus blochii) nutrient profile and standard mixed seafood broth composition. The dish contains golden pomfret, pork balls, prawns, clams, tomato, and broth. Per-100 g values reflect the composite edible portion including broth, consistent with HPB fish soup category benchmarks. Protein is elevated due to multiple protein sources; sodium is moderate-to-high reflecting broth preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood soup estimated from analogous low-GI protein-dominant broth-based soups; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Golden Pomfret Seafood Soup. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on analogous protein-dominant, broth-based seafood soups with minimal starchy carbohydrate content. Reference: Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 12.0) / 100 = 3.84, rounded to 3.8. Carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 2.4 × 5 = 12.0 g.

hypertension.guidance: A 500 g serving provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, which represents a substantial portion of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt broth or limit portion size to half a bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 32 and only 12 g of carbohydrate per standard 500 g serving, this soup has a low glycemic load of 3.8 and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added starchy accompaniments such as rice or noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from golden pomfret and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Golden pomfret is a source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which support cardiovascular health; the soup's low saturated fat content (0.6 g/100 g) and high protein-to-fat ratio make it a heart-friendly choice, though sodium from the broth should be monitored in patients with hypertensive cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Golden Pomfret Seafood Soup is a Singaporean Chinese-style broth-based seafood soup featuring slices of golden pomfret (Trachinotus blochii), a prized marine fish known for its mild, sweet flavour and firm white flesh. The soup is enriched with pork balls, fresh prawns, clams, and tomato, all simmered together in a clear, deeply flavoured fish broth infused with ginger and spring onion. The dish is a staple of Singapore's seafood soup hawker culture, commonly served at zi char stalls and seafood soup restaurants. The broth is light yet savoury, with a clean umami depth derived from the combination of fish, shellfish, and pork. Nutritionally, the soup is high in protein (approximately 7.8 g per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (2.4 g per 100 g), and provides a meaningful source of omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and calcium from the mixed seafood components. The glycemic index is low (estimated 32), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate-to-high due to the broth, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is dairy-free and gluten-free in its standard preparation. It is typically served as a main course in a bowl of approximately 500 g, often accompanied by steamed rice or plain rice on the side. The golden pomfret is particularly valued in Chinese culinary tradition for its perceived health benefits, including its richness in vitamins A, D, B3, B6, and B12.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for Golden Pomfret Seafood Soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore fish soup category benchmarks and USDA FoodData Central golden pomfret and mixed seafood component data. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and cross-referenced against two approved sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo fish soup category and USDA FoodData Central component data; no single approved-source entry exactly matches this composite dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace for this type of minimally processed seafood soup; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central seafood soup analogues. 3. GI is estimated at 32 (low) from analogous protein-dominant broth-based soups per Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from golden pomfret fatty acid profile per USDA FoodData Central. 5. Sodium of 420 mg/100 g (2100 mg per 500 g serving) reflects typical Singapore fish soup broth sodium levels; actual sodium may vary by restaurant preparation. 6. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork balls; is_halal is set to false. 7. Vitamin D is estimated at 2.5 mcg/100 g based on golden pomfret being an oily fish; exact value not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish. 8. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated values from approved sources where defensible; no -1 values remain in this record.
